request.question: Are both Warren County Canal and Dismal Swamp Canal located in the same state?

title: Warren County Canal
Great Dismal Swamp
Moses Grandy
Warren County Training School
Pungo River
Dismal Swamp Canal
Wallaceton (Chesapeake, Virginia)
Dismal Swamp State Park
Great Dismal Swamp National Wildlife Refuge
Wandilo, South Australia
sentences: The Warren County Canal was a branch of the Miami and Erie Canal in southwestern Ohio about 20 mi in length that connected the Warren County seat of Lebanon to the main canal at Middletown in the mid-19th century.

Lebanon was at the crossroads of two major roads, the highway from Cincinnati to Columbus (later U.S. Route 42) and the road from Chillicothe to the College Township (Oxford), but Lebanon businessmen and civic leaders wanted better transportation facilities and successfully lobbied for their own canal, part of the canal fever of the first third of the 19th century.
The Warren County Canal was never successful, operating less than a decade before the state abandoned it.
The Great Dismal Swamp is a large swamp in the Coastal Plain Region of southeastern Virginia and northeastern North Carolina, between Norfolk, Virginia, and Elizabeth City, North Carolina.
It is located in parts of the southern Virginia independent cities of Chesapeake and Suffolk and northern North Carolina counties of Gates, Pasquotank, and Camden.
Some estimates place the size of the original swamp at over 1 e6acre , stretching from Norfolk, Virginia to Edenton, North Carolina.
Moses Grandy (  1786 - unknown), was an African-American author, abolitionist, and, for more than the first four decades of his life, an enslaved person.
At eight years of age he became the property of his playmate, James Grandy and two years later he was hired out for work.
The monies Moses earned were collected and held until James Grandy turned 21.
Grandy helped build the Great Dismal Swamp Canal and learned how to navigate boats.
It was that skill that led him to be made commander of several boats that traveled the canal and Pasquotank River, transporting merchandise from Elizabeth City, North Carolina to Norfolk, Virginia.
The position allowed him to be better fed, shod and dressed.
Able to keep a portion of his earnings, Grandy arranged to buy his freedom twice and twice his owners kept the money and held him in slavery.
An arrangement was made for an honorable man to buy him and Grandy earned the money to buy his freedom a third time, this time successfully.
Warren County Training School is a historic Rosenwald School located near Wise, Warren County, North Carolina.
It was built in 1931, and is a large, one-story, nine classroom brick school.
It measures approximately 222 feet by 58 feet, with a rear wing measuring 42 feet by 59 feet.
Also on the property are the contributing teacherage (principal’s residence) (1925), brick cafeteria building (c. 1955), and brick agricultural building (c. 1955).
The complex continued to operate as a school until 1970.
The Warren County Training School is one of 25 schools that were constructed using Rosenwald funds in Warren County.
The Pungo River is a river in eastern North Carolina, United States.

It originally began in the Great Dismal Swamp in Washington County, North Carolina; the upper part of the river has since been supplanted by the Pungo River Canal, dug in the 1950s to improve drainage of local farmland.
The river flows southeast and forms part of the boundary between Beaufort County and Hyde County.
The river then widens dramatically, turns west, and flows past Belhaven, North Carolina before joining the Pamlico River near Pamlico Sound.
The Dismal Swamp Canal is located along the eastern edge of the Great Dismal Swamp in Virginia and North Carolina in the United States.
It is the oldest continually operating man-made canal in the United States, opened in 1805, and closed in October 2016.
It is part of the Intracoastal Waterway, an inland route, which parallels the east coast and offers boaters shelter from the Atlantic Ocean from Manasquan Inlet, New Jersey, to Brownsville, Texas.
The route runs through bays, lakes, rivers, streams, and canals, and includes the Intracoastal Waterway running from Norfolk, Virginia, to the Florida Keys.
Wallaceton is a historic home located at Chesapeake, Virginia.
The original section was built between 1853 and 1863, as a company store.
It was expanded after the American Civil War.
It is a 1 1/2-story, rectangular, Greek Revival style frame dwelling.
It has flat corner pilasters, a heavy box cornice under the eaves, and a full width front porch.
Also on the property are a contributing two-room kitchen building and a dairy.
About 1910, it was relocated approximately 100 feet to the east of the Dismal Swamp Canal to remove it from canal property.
It was named for John Gallaudet Wallace (1840-1910) a farmer and businessman who fought in the Civil War for the Confederacy, as a Captain of Company C, 61st Virginia Volunteer Infantry Regiment.
Dismal Swamp State Park is a North Carolina state park in Camden County, North Carolina in the United States.
The park was created as a state natural area in 1974 with the help of The Nature Conservancy, and on July 28, 2007 the NC General Assembly re-designated it as a state park.
It opened to the public in 2008.
This marked the first time that public access to Great Dismal Swamp was made possible in North Carolina.
The park covers 14432 acre of protected land on the North Carolina/Virginia border.
Park offices are three miles (5 km) south of the border on U.S. Route 17 near South Mills.
Features of the park include the canal which is used regularly by boaters using the Intracoastal Waterway and several miles of hiking and biking trails.

The Great Dismal Swamp National Wildlife Refuge was created in 1974 to help protect and preserve a portion of the Great Dismal Swamp, a marshy region on the Coastal Plain of southeastern Virginia and northeastern North Carolina between Norfolk, Virginia, and Elizabeth City, North Carolina in the United States.
It is located in parts of the independent cities of Chesapeake and Suffolk in Virginia, and the counties of Camden, Gates, and Pasquotank in North Carolina.
Wandilo is a settlement in the Australian state of South Australia.
At the 2011 census , Wandilo shared a population of 268 with the adjoining localities of Dismal Swamp and Wepar.
It was named after the railway station on the Mount Gambier railway line, and is recorded to mean "a swamp where native companions resort".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Features of the park include the canal which is used regularly by boaters using the Intracoastal Waterway and several miles of hiking and biking trails.

The Warren County Canal was never successful, operating less than a decade before the state abandoned it.

It originally began in the Great Dismal Swamp in Washington County, North Carolina; the upper part of the river has since been supplanted by the Pungo River Canal, dug in the 1950s to improve drainage of local farmland.  The river flows southeast and forms part of the boundary between Beaufort County and Hyde County.  The river then widens dramatically, turns west, and flows past Belhaven, North Carolina before joining the Pamlico River near Pamlico Sound.

It is located in parts of the independent cities of Chesapeake and Suffolk in Virginia, and the counties of Camden, Gates, and Pasquotank in North Carolina.

Wallaceton (Chesapeake, Virginia) | Wallaceton is a historic home located at Chesapeake, Virginia.  The original section was built between 1853 and 1863, as a company store.  It was expanded after the American Civil War.  It is a 1 1/2-story, rectangular, Greek Revival style frame dwelling.  It has flat corner pilasters, a heavy box cornice under the eaves, and a full width front porch.  Also on the property are a contributing two-room kitchen building and a dairy.  About 1910, it was relocated approximately 100 feet to the east of the Dismal Swamp Canal to remove it from canal property.  It was named for John Gallaudet Wallace (1840-1910) a farmer and businessman who fought in the Civil War for the Confederacy, as a Captain of Company C, 61st Virginia Volunteer Infantry Regiment.

Dismal Swamp, South Australia | Dismal Swamp is a locality in the Australian state of South Australia located about 361 km south-east of the state capital of Adelaide and about 19 km north-west of the municipal seat of Mount Gambier.

response.summary_1:
Primary topics: Dismal Swamp Canal; Warren County Canal

- Dismal Swamp Canal — passage [1] states it is "located along the eastern edge of the Great Dismal Swamp in Virginia and North Carolina".
- Warren County Canal — passage [3] states it was a branch of the Miami and Erie Canal in "southwestern Ohio" (connected Lebanon to Middletown).
- Ambiguity/missing facts: Dismal Swamp Canal spans two states (Virginia and North Carolina) — this makes "single-state" membership ambiguous; Warren County Canal is only attributed to Ohio. No other candidate canal pages or variant names are present in the passages.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
  1. No additional retrieval needed — passages provide the location facts for both canals.

Pungo River | The Pungo River is a river in eastern North Carolina, United States. It is located in parts of the independent cities of Chesapeake and Suffolk in Virginia, and the counties of Camden, Gates, and Pasquotank in North Carolina.

South Canal, Ohio | South Canal is a census-designated place (CDP) in Trumbull County, Ohio, United States.  The population was 1,346 at the 2000 census.  It is part of the Youngstown–Warren–Boardman, OH-PA Metropolitan Statistical Area.

Alligator River (North Carolina) | Alligator River is a small river in eastern North Carolina, separating Dare County and Tyrrell County.  It empties into Albemarle Sound.  A 21-mile canal connects the Alligator River with Pungo River to its west.  The Lindsey C. Warren Bridge of U.S. Route 64 crosses the river.  Many believe the Alligator River is named for its shape and mere connection to the swamp.

response.summary_2: No — Dismal Swamp Canal is located in Virginia and North Carolina, while the Warren County Canal was in southwestern Ohio.
